coredata.dc:title: ROS, pH, and magnetically responsive ZnFe2O4@l-Cysteine@NGQDs nanocarriers as charge-reversal drug delivery system for controlled and targeted cancer chemo-sonodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Gandomi, Faezeh
Rostami, Mojtaba
Ahmadi, Farhad
Mohammad Sorouri, Amir
Badiei, Alireza
Fasihi-Ramandi, Mahdi
Reza Ganjali, Mohammad
Ehrlich, Hermann
Rahimi-Nasrabadi, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we designed ZnFe2O4 nano-sonosensitizers (NSSs) was achieved through coating with biocompatible l-Cysteine (Cys) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). ZnFe2O4 @Cys@NGQDs nanocarrier was showed change of surface charge (in cancer cells) as well as the enhancement of the sonocatalytic property (for the production of radicals) by external and internal stimuli, with pH and ultrasound (US), respectively. The results show the increase in the anticancer activity of Curcumin (Cur) using ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs as an effective drug delivery system in chemo-sonodynamic therapy (CSDT). The results of the in vitro cytotoxic assay show a significant inhibition of A549 cells by ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs-Cur compared to the free form of Cur. The Cur drug loading efficiency of nano-carriers were as high as 61.3 % w/w for ZnFe2O4@Cys) and 72.2 % w/w for ZnFe2O4@Cys-NGQDs. The value of Cur release with US radiation after 96 h for ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs-Cur at pH = 5.5 was 89.17 %, which was higher than the value of release under the same conditions at pH = 7.4 (72.21 %).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Nano-sonosensitizers
Chemo-sonodynamic therapy
l-Cysteine
Curcumin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro anti-cancer drug loading of nano-carriers
In vitro evaluation of the Cur release behavior of the carriers with and without ultrasonic wave irradiation
In vitro drug loading and release studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Institute for Medical Research Development

Tehran University of Medical Sciences Center Cancer Research Institute

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Research reported in this publication was supported by Elite Researcher Grant Committee under award number (987933) from the National Institute for Medical Research Development (NIMAD), Tehran, Iran. MRN was supported by Alexander von Humboldt Foundation, Germany (Georg Forster research fellowship for experienced researchers program). HE acknowledge OPUS 19 grant of National Science Centre, Poland (2020/37/B/ST5/01909) as well as National Science Centre, Poland: MAESTRO 12 grant (2020/38/A/ST5/00151).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of (a) NGQDs, (b) l-Cysteine, (c) ZnFe2O4, and (d) ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs.

(a) FESEM images of the N-GQDs and (b) ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs nano-carriers with two magnification times (200 and 500 nm); (c) EDS spectrum of ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs and its atomic percentages of the elements; (d) SEM EDX element mappings of C, O, S, Fe, Zn and N of selected area; (e, f and h) HRTEM images with different magnification times of ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs; (g) SAED pattern of the ZnFe2O4@mZnO@N-GQDs.
FT-IR spectra of (a) Curcumin powder; (b) ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs-Cur; (c) ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs; and (d) ZnFe2O4@Cysteine.
(a) ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs, (b) ZnFe2O4@Cysteine and (c) ZnFe2O4 VSM magnetization curve.
(a, b and c). (a, b) Zeta potential measurement of ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs at different pH (5.5 and 7.4).
(a, c). N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of (a) ZnFe2O4@Cysteine, (c) ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs and (d) NGQDs the corresponding pore size distribution plots (b, d, e) (inset).
(a and b). (a) UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples solution; (b) comparison of PL spectra for NGQDs and ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs solution under excitation wavelength of 365 nm.
(a and b). Influence of on the cell viability of different amounts of ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs, ZnFe2O4@Cysteine, ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs-Cur, and Cur after (a) 24 and (b) 48 h of incubation.

Total release profiles for Cur-loaded nano-carriers at 37 °C through the dual ultrasound/pH responsive mechanism. The release experiments were performed at various intervals (t = 3, 5, 24, 48, 72 and 96 h) after 20 min ultrasound treatment at pH values of 7.4 or 5.5.
The electrostatic interactions of ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs nano-carriers surface with normal and cancer cell membrane.

Schematic illustration of the synthesis route of ZnFe2O4@l-Cysteine@NGQDs and principle for ZnFe2O4@l-Cysteine@NGQDs-enhanced chemo-sonodynamic combined therapy employing ZnFe2O4 NPs as the sonosensitizers.
Coercivity, remanence, and saturation magnetization of the products.
The percentage of drug loading efficiency of nano-carriers after 24 h.

head.ce:title: ROS, pH, and magnetically responsive ZnFe2O4@l-Cysteine@NGQDs nanocarriers as charge-reversal drug delivery system for controlled and targeted cancer chemo-sonodynamic therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we designed ZnFe2O4 nano-sonosensitizers (NSSs) was achieved through coating with biocompatible l-Cysteine (Cys) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). ZnFe2O4 @Cys@NGQDs nanocarrier was showed change of surface charge (in cancer cells) as well as the enhancement of the sonocatalytic property (for the production of radicals) by external and internal stimuli, with pH and ultrasound (US), respectively. The results show the increase in the anticancer activity of Curcumin (Cur) using ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs as an effective drug delivery system in chemo-sonodynamic therapy (CSDT). The results of the in vitro cytotoxic assay show a significant inhibition of A549 cells by ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs-Cur compared to the free form of Cur. The Cur drug loading efficiency of nano-carriers were as high as 61.3 % w/w for ZnFe2O4@Cys) and 72.2 % w/w for ZnFe2O4@Cys-NGQDs. The value of Cur release with US radiation after 96 h for ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs-Cur at pH = 5.5 was 89.17 %, which was higher than the value of release under the same conditions at pH = 7.4 (72.21 %).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tumor-specific treatment, avoiding toxic effects on the normal tissues [1], is key in cancer treatment. Consequently, different microenvironment-based treatment approaches based on pH, redox reaction, hypoxia, and immune responses have been developed and evaluated [2,3]. The resulting response in these methods is, however, greatly dependent on the type and/or stage of the tumor, as well as, each patient’s individual response [4]. A parallel approach is focusing the energy of outer sources on tumors using external triggers, which enhance the controllability and specificity of the corresponding techniques.

US waves, which have been widely used in clinical diagnostic imaging, are also known for therapeutic functions, as in tumor ablation using focused high-intensity ultrasound waves [5]. US can be used to activate nano-sonosensitizers (NSSs) to form toxic reactive oxygen species (ROS), in a so-called sonodynamic therapy (SDT) approach, similar to the common photodynamic therapy (PDT) based on laser [6,7]. SDT enjoys advantages of noninvasiveness and high penetration in tissues. Yet, the clinical function of SDT is limited by the efficiency of NSSs. For instance, traditional organic sonosensitizers like photofrin, ATX-70, and chlorophyll derivatives, which are known to leave the body quickly, have been further reported to have low stability [8]. In comparison, physiochemical properties of inorganic nanomaterials excel those of traditional ones, and can hence be used as more efficient NSSs. Examples of the commonest inorganic NSSs include ZnO, TiO2, and ZnFe2O4 nano-particles (NPs), which lead to the generation of ROS like singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide radical ( . O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) [9]. These NSSs are, however, not without shortcomings and have low quantum yields in generating ROS reducing the electron (e−)/ hole (h+) pair-separation and hence adversely influencing their SDT efficiency. Preventing the recombination of ultrasound triggered electron/hole pairs in the semiconducting NSSs is hence a challenge. Ultrasound based SDT through activating NSSs is recently receiving great attention as a tool for treating the deep-tissue tumors. The method can both damage tumors through forming ROS, and enhancement of the uptake and cytotoxicity of chemotherapy agents [10].

Traditional anticancer drugs have the serious problems including low solubility in water, low selectivity leading to damages to healthy tissues, and low life times in the body. Thus, NSSs like magnetic ZnFe2O4 are receiving more attention in drug delivery nanosystem (DDNS) due to advantages like enhanced sonodynamic chemo-therapeutic activity resulting from improved solubility of hydrophobic drugs (such as Cur), longer half-lives, minimizing potential immunogenicity, and sustainable and controllable release of pharmaceutical agents [11,12]. Such nanostructures can also passively accumulate in the tumor tissue due to their enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) properties helping avoid multi-drug resistance [13]. Researchers active in developing efficient nanocarriers, are very interested in ZnFe2O4 NSSs due to their stability, nontoxicity, biocompatibility and non-immunogenicity as well as selective inherent cytotoxicity against cancer [14,15].

One drawback of NSSs is their agglomeration arising from high surface energy. Capping techniques have hence been used for avoiding the increased size of the agglomerates, stabilizing the size of the particles in mixtures, and better yet, enhancing their target specificity in treatments [16,17]. The commonest capping agents or stabilizers used for modifying ZnFe2O4 NSSs include hexamethylenetetramine, polyethyleneimine [18], 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid [19], polyethylene glycol [20], polyacrylamide grafted guar gum polymer [21], and Cys, which has more recently been used in this application given its low toxicity, stability and robust fluorescence behavior [22]. Magnetic ferrites NPs for example, NiFe2O4, MnFe2O4, CuFe2O4 and CoFe2O4 are important in the magnetic resonance imaging and medical applications [23–25]. Among all magnetic ferrites NPs, ZnFe2O4 is preferred for biomedical drug delivery due to its low toxicity and high biocompatibility (at concentrations below 125 μg/mL) [26–28].

On the other hand, GQDs are known for unique properties such as high quantum yield (QY) fluorescence emission, considerable photo stability, stability in water, non-cytotoxicity and biocompatibility, GQDs are hence considered as very promising materials for various biotechnological and medical applications [29]. Instances of GQDs applications include their use in light-emitting diodes, photovoltaic devices, bio- imaging probes and photodynamic therapy [30–32]. Based on the qualities and application of GQDs, they hold promise for use in drug delivery.

In the light of the above, the present work focused on integrating zero dimensional (0D) NGQDs with ZnFe2O4 NSSs to obtain modified CSDT for treating cancer. The resulting sonosensitizer was expected to benefit from the high electroconductivity and surface chemistry, as well as, the considerable capability of NGQDs in creating photothermal reactions. For this purpose NGQDs were directly grown on Cys, followed by in situ growth of ZnFe2O4 NPs (ZnFe2O4@ Cys@NGQDs nano-carriers) for DDS. This ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs create DDS-guided synergistic CSDT in anticancer treatments. One reason is the effective separation of the e−/h+ pairs formed through US inducing irradiation. The high drug encapsulation efficiency of Cys and NGQDs can also synergistically enhance the CSDT efficacy of SDT. Further, the integration of ZnFe2O4 NSSs with Cys@NGQDs creates enhanced dual stimuli of ROS/pH-responsive agents for DDS under sonodynamic and chemotherapy [33].

Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, C2H5OH, urea, C6H8O7·H2O (citric acid monohydrate (CA)), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), cetyl trimethyl-ammonium bromide (CTAB), and Cys (C3H7NO2S) were purchased from Merck Co. 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), phosphate buffered solution (PBS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. A Smart-2-Pure ultra-pure water system (TKA Co, Germany) was used for supplying the deionized water used in all experiments. The A549 cell was obtained from the National Cell Bank of Iran (NCBI, Tehran). The chemicals were used without further processing at the laboratory.

Sol-gel is a suitable method for preparing ferrite nanostructures with desired nanoarchitecture. [34,35]. The ZnFe2O4 NPs were prepared through the so-called sol–gel techniques [36] followed by coating an Cys layer on the ZnFe2O4 NPs through the ultrasonic-assisted chemical co-precipitation. The formation of a surface chemical bond of Fe-S between ZnFe2O4 NPs and sulfur from Cys was don. Typically, ZnFe2O4 NPs (1 g), Cys (0.5 g,) and CTAB (0.03 g) were add-mixed in deionized water while stirring, followed by adding 25 mL of a 1.5 M sodium hydroxide solution (adjust the pH at 11) and an hour of stirring before moving the solution to an ultrasonic bath at 200 W and 20 kHz. During this 20 min treatment the pH of the solution was adjusted at 12. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min, and the separated solid product was repeatedly rinsed using absolute ethanol, and deionized water.

The typical procedure involved dissolving 2 g of CA in 30 mL of deionized water containing 5 g of urea, followed by thermal treatment of the solution in a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave at 180 °C for 48 h. The brown product (NGQDs) was separated by centrifugation and washed several times with distilled water and ethanol.

1 g of ZnFe2O4@Cys was admixed with a mixture of 5 wt% of NGQDs or GQDs in water and kept stirring for 1 day under ambient conditions to form a homogeneous solution, and the produced ZnFe2O4@Cys@5 wt% NGQDs was separated through centrifuging for 20 min at 7000 rpm and desiccated at 70 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-cancer drug loading of nano-carriers
ce:section.ce:para:
A precipitation procedure in acetone was used to form Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@Cys@5 wt% NGQDs nano-carriers. This involved gradually adding a solution of 1 mg of Cur in 10 mL acetone to 50 mL of a mixture of 60 mg of ZnFe2O4@Cys@5 wt% NGQDs nano-carriers in deionized water. The mixing was kept under magnetic stirring at room temperature until the acetone content was evaporated. The product was admixed with water, centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 20 min, rinsed with deionized water and left to dry under ambient conditions. The Cur content of the supernatant solution was determined at 420 nm by UV–Vis, and the data were used to determine Cur drug loading efficiency (DLE) based on the following equation (1):
(1)
D L E
%
=
t o t a l
C u r - s u p e r n a t a n t
f r e e
a m o u n t
o f
C u r
t o t a l
C u r
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro evaluation of the Cur release behavior of the carriers with and without ultrasonic wave irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
The typical experiment involved suspending a quantity of ZnFe2O4@Cys@5 wt% NGQDs-Cur nano-carriers in a certain volume of the release media which is mentioned below. A typical experiment was performed by dispersing 6 mg of Cur-loaded nano-carriers in 50 mL of 0.01 M PBS solutions (pH = 7. 4 or 5.5) at 37 °C and containing 1 % (v/v) Tween 80. Then, 4 mL of the above mixture solution sampled in the times of 3, 5, 24, 48, 72 and 96 h and release was measured after 30 min centrifuge at 5000 rpm. To compensate for the volume loss of the sample mixture, 4 mL of fresh PBS was added to it after each sampling. The concentration of Cur in the suspension was determined by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) at 420 nm. To evaluate the effect of ultrasonic waves on Cur release, the above method is used, except 20 min irradiation of the beakers before the time of 3, 5, 24, 48, 72 and 96 h. Total release percentage of Cur was calculated using the following expression (2):
(2)
T o t a l
r e l e a s e
%
o f
C u r =
∑
t = 0
t
M t
M 0
Mt, M0 and t being the total amount of the released Cur, the initial weight of Cur-loaded of the carriers, and the time of release of Cur, respectively.

The cytotoxicity of ZnFe2O4@Cys@5 wt% NGQDs and ZnFe2O4@Cys nano-carriers in the absence and presence of Cur on the human lung cancer cell line (A549 cells) was assessed using the MTT assay. The cell line was grown in RPMI 1640 medium (Gibco), supplemented with 10 % (v/v) FBS, 100 IU/mL of penicillin and 100 μg/mL streptomycin. The incubated cells were stored at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, until an 85 %confluence was reached. At this stage, a 0.25 % trypsin/EDTA solution was used to harvest the cells.

The cytotoxicity of the extract on the A549 cells was assessed using an MTT assay, and the percentages of the viable cells were measured through the formation of a purple formazon crystal from yellow tetrazolium salt by mitochondrial dehydrogenase.

The collected cells were plated at a density of 104 cells/well in a Nunc 96-well plate, and treated using different concentrations of the nanocarriers i.e. 2500, 1250, 625, 313, 156, 78, 39, µg/mL. 24 and 48 h after treatment, to each well was added the MTT solution (3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide). Negative and positive control samples were prepared using the culture media and 5 % triton X-100, and the incubation of the plates at 37 °C, continued for 180 min. After the incubation, the supernatant in each well, was replaced with 100 μL of DMSO and a further 20 min incubation was performed. The ELISA plate reader was used at λ = 570 nm, and the viability and cytotoxicity percentages were determined as below [37]:
(3)
V i a b i l i t y
%
=
m e a n
a b s o r b a n c e
o f
t o x i c a n t - O D
b l a n k
m e a n
a b s o r b a n c e
o f
n e g a t i v e
c o n t r o l - O D
b l a n k
× 100
(4)
c y t o t o x i c i t y
%
= 1 -
m e a n
a b s o r b a n c e
o f
t o x i c a n t
m e a n
a b s o r b a n c e
o f
n e g a t i v e
c o n t r o l
× 100

A multiwave ultrasonic homogenizer (Sonicator 3200; Ultrasonic Technology Co.; Tehran, Iran) was used to study the effect of US on the release of drug. A Philips X’ pert Pro MPD XRD with a graphite-filtered Cu Kα (k = 0.154 nm) radiation was used for the phase analysis of the samples. The studies were performed in the 2θ range of 10 to 90 at a step size of 0.021, the measuring time being 0.8 s per point. Further morphological and structural evaluations of the solid samples were performed through FESEM analyses on a Tescan MIRA3 FEG-SEM equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS), and HRTEM (HR-TEM FEI TECNAI F20) of the nanocomposites. The FT-IR spectrophotometery of the samples was performed on a Nicolet Magna IR 550 spectrometer. Evaluation of room temperature magnetic properties was performed using a PMC MicroMag 3900 VSM with a 1 Tesla magnet, Magnetic Daghigh Daneshpajouh Co (Iran). Drug loading and release of Cur were determined based on the data obtained from ultraviolet–visible array spectrometry using a UV–vis (Laboratory Industrial and Research System Co.,Tehran, Iran). A Malvern Instruments Zetasizer, Zeta Potential (ZP) Analyzer was used for zeta potential measurements. The respective specific surface area and pore size distribution values of the composites were determined through the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods, using an automatic micropore and a Sensiran Co. chemisorption analyzer (Iran).

The XRD patterns of (a) NGQDs, (b) Cys, (c) ZnFe2O4, and (d) ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs nano-carriers can be observed in Fig. 1 . The XRD pattern of NGQDs included a broad peak at 2θ = 20-26°, corresponding to the (002) peak in JCPDS Card No, 75–0444 [38]. The XRD patterns of Cys includes peaks around 18.77, 21.87, 28.62, 33.06, 34.49, 38.37, 42.12, 44.77, 46.55, 65.27, 80.45° which were correlated with the (111), (101), (121), (220), (202), (301), (103), (231), (132), (240) and (332) crystal planes of Cys (JCPDS Card No, 40–1289), respectively [39].The XRD pattern of pure ZnFe2O4 includes representative diffraction peaks at 19, 30.1, 35.3, 37.1, 43.0, 53.5, 56.3, 62.4, 70.6, 74.5 and 76.1° which comply those of the magnetic cubic structured ZnFe2O4 (JCPDS No. 22–1012) [40]. The diffraction peaks correspond to the respective (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440), (620), (533) and (444) crystal planes [41]. The peak at 2θ values of 34.2 and 50.3° indicate the formation of Fe2O3 with reference to the data in JCPDS No. 02–1047 [42]. The diffraction peaks of Cys at 28.62, 31.9, 39.8, 42.7 and 67.8° are showed in ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs, indicating that ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs was successfully prepared and that the crystalline forms of ZnFe2O4 and Cys didn’t change when the ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs nanocomposites were synthesized [43] . Therefore, the crystal structure of ZnFe2O4 NPs is stable after the coating of Cys and NGQDs. Moreover, the strong intensity of the diffraction peaks suggests that both pure ZnFe2O4 NPs and Cys are well crystallized [44]. Reducing the intensity of ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs nanocomposites peaks relative to ZnFe2O4 NPs and Cys indicates that the formation of Cys and NGQDs layer around ZnFe2O4 NPs have been successful.

The microstructure of N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@Cys@N-GQDs samples were evaluated through FESEM analyses. The FESEM images shows that the N-GQDs and ZnFe2O4@Cys@N-GQDs samples were composed of uniformly sized spheres with average sizes of 4 ± 1 nm (Fig. 2 (a)) and the particle size of ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs nano-carriers is determined to be 80–170 nm with near-spherical shape (Fig. 2b). EDS mapping and HRTEM of ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs were represented in Fig. 2 c-e. In the SEM Figure, the nanoparticles seem to be agglomerated which could be due to adhesion of particles to each other. EDS mapping (Fig. 2d) and EDX (Fig. 2c) clearly indicated that C, O, S, Fe, Zn and N were uniformly dispersed on the sample surface.

The HRTEM results obtained for ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs shows the formation of spherical shape nanostructures (Fig. 2e, f) and prove that ZnFe2O4 NPs are uniformly coated with Cys and NGQDs. Based on the HRTEM images, the ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs and NGQDs have an average diameter of about 100 and 5 nm, respectively. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) also reveals the high crystallinity of ZnFe2O4 (inset of Fig. 2g). In the SAED the lattice fringes with an d-spacing of 0.35 and 0.24 nm associated to the (002) and (1120) planes, of sp2 graphitic carbon in NGQDs. The inter-planar spacing are 0.21, 0.25 and 0.29 nm attributing to the (400), (311) and (220) fringes of ZnFe2O4 NPs (Fig. 2 (h)). Furthermore, the ZnFe2O4 NPs diffraction rings of SAED pattern shown in Fig. 2 (g), are corresponded to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) planes, respectively. SAED and HRTEM image results are consistent with XRD, which indicates the successful synthesis of ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs nano-carriers.

Fig. 3 illustrates the FT-IR spectra of (a) Cur powder; (b) ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs-Cur; (c) ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs; and (d) ZnFe2O4@Cy. The FT-IR spectrum of ZnFe2O4 includes the characteristic peaks of Fe-O with a band centered at 580 cm−1 [45]. In the case of NGQDs, the strong and wide bands at 3419 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of OH [46]. The symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of COOH groups merged with the stretching vibrations of CC are reflected by the two strong peaks at 1393 cm−1 and 1600–1800 cm−1 [47–49]. In the case of Cur the FT-IR spectrum includes characteristic bands corresponding to the OH group of the phenolic hydroxyl group, at 3500 cm−1, the stretching bands of benzene CC at 1627 cm−1 and 1515 cm−1, and the stretching of the CH bonds in the aromatic ring at 856 cm−1 [50–52]. The FT-IR spectra of the cur-loaded nano-carriers, included both peaks of the nanocarrier and curcumin, which indicates the successful synthesis of the cur-loaded nano-carriers. Further the band at 2094 cm−1 (SH) was not observed for ZnFe2O4@Cys or ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs, reflecting the binding of Cys onto the surface of ZnFe2O4 NPs through forming a covalent Fe-S bond [53]. Hence, it is clear that the Cys molecules bind directly to the ZnFe2O4 via Fe-S covalent bonds.

The VSM is used to measure the magnetic properties of nanomaterials such as coercivity (Hc), remanence (Mr), and saturation magnetization (Ms). The width of the hysteresis loop indicates the hardness of the magnetic nanomaterial [54]. For example, narrow loops correspond to soft magnets, and wide loops correspond to hard magnets. The magnetization curve of (a) ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs, (b) ZnFe2O4@Cys and (c) ZnFe2O4 were determined at room temperature by VSM analysis as shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4, evidently shows a typical hysteresis loop in the magnetic behavior of three samples, reflecting them as being soft magnetic materials [55]. It is worth noting that the coating of non-magnetic Cys and NGQD on the surface of ZnFe2O4 NPs leads to the reduction of Ms in the respective nanocomposites [33]. The magnetic property of the nanocarrier presente possible to control it through an external magnet for targeted delivery to the tumor tissue, which is very valuable in drug delivery [56]. The Hc, remanence Mr, and Ms data for three samples are presented in Table 1 .

Fig. 6 (a, b, c, d) illustrate the curves of N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore width distribution for NGQDs, ZnFe2O4@Cys and ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs samples. The IV isotherms with the H3 hysteresis loop, observed for three samples reflect their nanoporous behaviors [60]. The BET surface area (SBET) and pore volume (PV) of ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs were 43.69 m2/g and 0.101515 cm3/g, which were more than those of ZnFe2O4@Cys (26.05 m2/g and 0.061154 cm3/g) (Fig. 6 (a, c)). The BJH pore size distribution values were determined from the N2 isotherms and are as small as 3.25, 3.74 and 3.78 nm for ZnFe2O4@Cys, ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs and NGQDs, respectively (Fig. 6 (b, d)). The SBET and the PV for NGQDs are 5.6 m2/g and 0.036 cm3/g, respectively. Therefore, the coating of ZnFe2O4 NPs with Cys and NGQDs provide an excellent platform increases the surface area for drug loading and delivery.

UV–Visible absorption spectra of the samples solution are shown in Fig. 7 (a). Cys depicted clear absorption peak centered at 234 nm [61]. NGQDs shows three absorption peaks at 236, 345 and 383 nm which belonged to π → π* (CC bond), n → π* (CO bond) and n → π* (CN bond) electron transitions, respectively [62]. In addition, the Fig. 7 (a) spectrum reported in Cur reveals a broad band centered at 290 and 425 nm which assigned to π → π* type excitation of its extended π-conjugation system [63]. PL emission spectrum of NGQDs and ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs solution were obtained at excitation of 365 nm (inset of Fig. 7b). PL emission spectroscopy is used for understanding the electron (e−)/hole (h+) pair separation and recombination behavior of sonoinduced charge carriers. PL spectra shows that the samples have blue emission at 456 nm, mainly originated from the π → π* and n → π* electron transitions. The intensity of ZnFe2O4@Cysteine@NGQDs PL peak has decreased compared to NGQDs, which indicates a lower charge carrier recombination rate and an increase in charge carrier separation [64].

According to ISO 10993–5, cell viability percentages over 80 % are considered non-cytotoxic; while the respective values of 80 %–60 %, 60 %–40 %, and < 40 % are defined as weakly, moderately, strongly cytotoxic [65,66]. The biocompatibility of the developed drug-delivery system was determined through MTT assay against human lung cancer cells (A549), and it was found that in the case of all samples increasing the concentration lowered the cell viability. Fig. 8 a, shows that after 24 h of incubation in the range of 0–250 µg/mL both ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs-Cur nano-carriers and Cur had good biocompatibility values. The cell viability remained over 80 % after 24 h in the case of the Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs, which meant that the nano-carriers could be safely used for drug delivery. However, Fig. 8b, indicates that Cur solutions and Cur loaded ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs carriers had a dose-dependent cytotoxicity behavior, and higher concentrations of the free or carriers loaded drugs led to increased damage to A549 cells. The cell viability of free Cur solutions was found to be higher than that of ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs-Cur, which was attributed to the faster release profile in the case of the Cur-loaded ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs nanocarriers, which can be complied with the high accumulation values obtained through ZP measurement (Scheme 1). Accordingly ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs can act as a safe Cur delivery, over the tested concentration range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug loading and release studies
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis spectrometry was used to determine the Cur loading efficiency of the ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs nano-carrier; the obtained values are presented in Table 2 . Drug loading efficiency of ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs was interestingly high because Cur loaded via H-bonding, π–π stacking interaction and electrostatic interaction conjugation (DLE = 72.20 %). The high drug loading in ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs can be related to its high porosity due to the dispersion of NGQDs in the matrix, electrostatic interactions (π–π stacking) and formation of hydrogen bonds between the polar groups present in cur (–OH and CO) and ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs (OH, CO, SH and NH2) [67]. The BET results and Table 2 show that with the addition of NGQD in the ZnFe2O4@Cys ​​matrix, the surface area and the amount of drug loading increases.

The release of Cur was studied at 37 °C in two different pH values of 7.4, and 5.5 that are similar to the pH of normal and tumor cells with and without US radiation, and the results indicated a sustained release of Cur. Fig. 9 indicates that the release of Cur was higher in the simulated tumor tissue conditions (52.12 % without US radiation) as opposed to the normal physiological conditions (38.50 % without US radiation). Protonation of amide groups formed between NGQD and Cys in the acidic environment of tumor tissues causes separation of amide bonds and loss of porosity of the amide network, resulting in increased Cur release [68,69]. As seen in Fig. 9, the amount of drug release at pH 5.5 (89.17 %) and 7.4 (72.20 %) with ultrasonic wave radiation was increased. Compared the without US radiation targeted drug delivery approach, the microbubble cavitation effect, can be enhanced the amount of drug release [70].

0D NGQDs and Cys were coupled with ZnFe2O4 NSSs (ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs nanocarriers) to obtain a chemo-therapeutic probe with enhanced sono-catalytic efficiency against tumors. The mechanism was inspired by principles of semiconductor physics and photocatalysis chemistry, and was based on the using external US activation together with the considerably electro-conductive NGQDs for formation and stabilization of electrons/and holes pairs. When subjected to US, ZnFe2O4 NSSs produced considerable quantities of ROS causing mitochondrial damage. In addition, the π–π interactions of conjugated aromatic rings present in NGQDs and the benzene ring present in Cur could also influence the Cur loading of ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs nanocarriers. In comparison to the recent reports on external stimuli-responsive nanocarriers, ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs-Cur enjoyed distinct merits like controllable drug release, considerable safety that make it potentially useful for clinical applications. The improved cytotoxicity of the procedure can be due to the better permeability through biomembranes and the enhanced intracellular uptake of Cur as a result of Cur combined with the cytotoxic properties of ROS. It could hence be concluded that the CSDT based on ZnFe2O4@Cys@NGQDs-Cur offers a safe and effective procedure for tumor treatment.

Faezeh Gandomi: Methodology. Mojtaba Rostami: Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Visualization, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Farhad Ahmadi: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Methodology. Amir Mohammad Sorouri: Methodology. Alireza Badiei: Resources, Supervision. Mohammad Reza Ganjali: Writing - review & editing. Hermann Ehrlich: Writing - review & editing. Mehdi Rahimi-Nasrabadi: Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Research reported in this publication was supported by Elite Researcher Grant Committee under award number (987933) from the National Institute for Medical Research Development (NIMAD), Tehran, Iran. MRN was supported by Alexander von Humboldt Foundation, Germany (Georg Forster research fellowship for experienced researchers program). HE acknowledge OPUS 19 grant of National Science Centre, Poland (2020/37/B/ST5/01909) as well as National Science Centre, Poland: MAESTRO 12 grant (2020/38/A/ST5/00151).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.110544.
